meta.title: Realtime feed leaks sockets and listeners
meta.summary: A live feed gets slower after route changes because each mount adds another socket listener and some never clean up.
signals: Duplicate messages appear in the UI
Connection count keeps increasing in dev tools

context.symptom: A trading feed works for the first visit, but after opening and closing the page several times, each incoming event renders multiple duplicates.
context.userImpact: The page becomes noisy and slower over time, especially for users who navigate between dashboards.
context.environment: React route mounts a feed component that opens a WebSocket and binds message listeners inside an effect.

evidence.title: Heap growth
evidence.body: Heap grows by roughly 8MB after each open-close cycle and never returns to baseline.

evidence.title: Connection count
evidence.body: Network tab shows 1 socket, then 2, then 3 active connections after repeated visits.

evidence.title: Effect excerpt

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What explains the leak most directly?
stages.helperText: Choose the explanation that matches duplicate events and growing connection count.

options.label: The effect recreates sockets and listeners without proper cleanup, so mounts and dependency changes accumulate subscriptions.
options.feedback: Correct. The growing connection count and duplicate events both point to incomplete teardown.

options.label: Message payloads are just too large, so the browser reports a memory leak.
options.feedback: Large payloads can stress memory, but they do not explain steadily increasing connections and duplicate listeners.

options.label: React reconciliation is leaking DOM nodes even though subscriptions are cleaned correctly.
options.feedback: The evidence points to retained external resources, not DOM diffing itself.

options.label: StrictMode double-invocation is the issue, so production cleanup does not matter.
options.feedback: StrictMode can reveal lifecycle issues, but the real problem is missing cleanup around external subscriptions.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three most effective debug steps first.
stages.helperText: Start by reproducing lifecycle churn and proving retained subscriptions.

candidates.label: Repeat mount-unmount cycles and count active connections or listeners after each pass.
Inspect the effect dependencies and whether cleanup removes listeners and closes the socket.
Use dev tools or logs to confirm old handlers still receive events after unmount.
Compress socket payloads before checking lifecycle behavior.
Raise browser memory thresholds so the app stops slowing down.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the first reliable fix?
stages.helperText: Choose the options that reduce retained subscriptions and align lifecycle ownership.

options.label: Return cleanup that removes listeners and closes the socket on dependency change or unmount.
options.feedback: Correct. External subscriptions must be torn down explicitly.

options.label: Limit the effect to the true connection key so unrelated state changes do not recreate the socket.
options.feedback: Correct. Broad dependency churn often creates accidental reconnection storms.

options.label: Centralize socket ownership or guard post-unmount state updates so only one active subscription exists.
options.feedback: Correct. Ownership clarity prevents overlapping listeners across mounts.

options.label: Suppress duplicate message warnings in the console to keep the UI calm.
options.feedback: That hides the signal instead of fixing the retained subscriptions.

options.label: Create the socket in render so React always keeps it in sync with props.
options.feedback: Opening sockets during render makes lifecycle bugs worse, not better.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best follow-up guardrail?
stages.helperText: Pick the safeguard that catches leaks before users notice them.

options.label: Add a mount-unmount regression test and connection-count instrumentation so duplicate subscriptions are obvious.
options.feedback: Correct. This guards the lifecycle bug directly and provides a production signal if it returns.

options.label: Track only feed latency because slower messages caused the duplicates.
options.feedback: Latency is useful, but it does not catch duplicate listeners or retained sockets.

options.label: Have someone manually refresh the page monthly to watch for memory growth.
options.feedback: Manual checks are too weak for a lifecycle leak that can recur with small code changes.

options.label: Force a full page reload after every navigation away from the feed.
options.feedback: That hides the leak by resetting the app instead of fixing subscription ownership.

scoreBands.label: Leak identified too vaguely
scoreBands.summary: You saw slowness, but not the retained subscription lifecycle behind it.

scoreBands.label: Correct diagnosis, softer guardrail
scoreBands.summary: You found the cleanup issue, but the debug order or long-term guard missed one lever.

scoreBands.label: Lifecycle ownership mindset
scoreBands.summary: You connected the leak to subscription teardown, controlled dependencies, and added a durable guard.

idealRunbook: Measure active connections and retained listeners.
Fix cleanup and dependency scope together.
Add instrumentation that makes duplicate subscriptions visible.

teachingBlocks.title: Common interview trap
teachingBlocks.text: Many candidates say 'memory leak' but stop there. The stronger answer names what is retained and why the lifecycle allows it.

teachingBlocks.title: Stronger explanation
items: Cleanup is tied to the same effect that subscribed.
Dependencies reflect connection identity, not every render input.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If the feed connection were shared globally, how would your cleanup strategy change?
